title: Challenging Cases in Nutritional and Medical Treatment of Obesity

items.title: Why providers should estimate daily energy expenditure
items.text: Many patients believe only highly restrictive diets succeed, leading to frustration. Reasonable estimates of daily energy expenditure enable sustainable dietary changes rather than repeated futile very-low-calorie attempts.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "By being more familiar with the components and how to estimate daily energy expenditure, providers can help patients avoid repeated, futile diet attempts and help them select approaches that, by being more moderate, are more sustainable."

items.title: How to raise obesity without harming the therapeutic relationship
items.text: Link lifestyle choices to adiposity-related comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia) rather than implying the patient is unhealthy because they are 'too fat.' If the patient is unwilling to change, defer the conversation.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Pointing out that lifestyle choices are leading to health problems is often less threatening to the patient than implying that they are unhealthy because they are too fat"

items.title: Why self-monitoring is central to long-term weight-loss maintenance
items.text: Self-monitoring is the cognitive behavioral technique most strongly associated with successful maintenance. Food intake monitoring requires near-constant attention; dietary recall is notoriously inaccurate, especially in people with overweight/obesity.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The cognitive behavioral technique most strongly associated with long-term, successful weight-loss maintenance is self-monitoring."

items.title: How to use a two-week food record effectively
items.text: Ask patients to record everything for 2 weeks to reveal true intake patterns. Emphasize the diary is for the patient's benefit—not to please the clinician—to reduce socially desirable reporting.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "I find it helpful to ask patients to record everything they eat for 2 weeks as a way for them to understand the value of doing so."

items.title: Estimating walking and spontaneous activity energy cost
items.text: Walking calculators using distance and body weight are accurate because human exercise efficiency is narrow (20–30% of oxygen consumption relative to work). Three miles daily walking may expend ~300 kcal; housework may add 100–700 kcal.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "This woman walks at least 3 miles per day to get to and from work, which will expend 300 kcal/day."

items.title: Why low TEF cannot explain failure to lose weight on perceived low intake
items.text: TEF averages ~10% of ingested energy (range 2–20%). Even at the lowest TEF, the deficit is far too small to explain lack of weight loss on a perceived 1200 kcal/day intake when expenditure is higher.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "At the very lowest end of possible TEF, she would expend only 94 kcal less per day than we would predict based on the 10% TEF rule, which cannot account for her failure to lose weight on a perceived intake of 1200 kcal/day."

items.title: Night eating syndrome diagnostic criteria
items.text: Diagnosis requires evening hyperphagia or nocturnal ingestion plus ≥3 of: morning anorexia, urge to eat after dinner/at night, insomnia, evening mood worsening, or belief that eating is needed to return to sleep. Prevalence ~1.5% in the US.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Night eating syndrome is also diagnosed if there are at least 3 of the following symptoms: (1) morning anorexia; (2) a strong urge to eat between dinner and sleep and/or during the night"

items.title: Why medication lists must include OTC agents in weight management
items.text: Many medications promote weight gain; substitution with weight-neutral alternatives is often possible. Appetite-stimulating drugs make comprehensive lifestyle programs much harder to sustain.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "When patients take medications that increase their appetite, it makes it loudly difficult to lose weight and keep it off."

items.title: How H1-receptor blockade promotes weight gain
items.text: Hypothalamic histamine suppresses feeding via H1R. H1 antagonists/inverse agonists (older antihistamines, some antipsychotics) increase food intake; H1 affinity correlates with orexigenic potency.
items.reference: Case 2: "H1R antagonists/inverse agonists (older over-the-counter antiallergy medications) are associated with increased food intake and weight gain."

items.title: Why exogenous glucocorticoids increase appetite
items.text: Exogenous steroids suppress hypothalamic POMC and α-MSH production, unmasking NPY/AgRP-driven appetite stimulation—the opposite of Addison disease with extreme anorexia from POMC overproduction.
items.reference: Case 2: "Exogenous corticosteroids suppress proopiomelanocortin, and thereby α-MSH production, resulting in an unopposed neuropeptide Y/agouti-related peptide effect to stimulate appetite."

items.title: Medications contributing to iatrogenic weight gain in Case 2
items.text: Amitriptyline, depot medroxyprogesterone, and diphenhydramine (Tylenol PM) are all associated with weight gain. Stopping them is not guaranteed to reverse weight gain but substitution may help.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: A, B, and C) Tricyclic antidepressant amitriptyline she takes to treat neuralgia and depression and depot medroxyprogesterone she uses for contraception and Tylenol PM she takes 5 times per week to help fall asleep"

items.title: How to counsel on commercial weight-loss programs
items.text: Some commercial programs have adequate published data. Weight Watchers offers lower-cost moderate restriction; Jenny Craig, Medifast, and OPTIFAST are more expensive and may use severe restriction.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Weight Watchers provide lower cost, moderately energy-restricted options, whereas Jenny Craig, Medifast, and OPTIFAST are more expensive and may employ severe energy-restricted approaches."

items.question: A woman reports eating ~1200 kcal/day without weight loss. She walks 3 miles daily and is active with housework. She denies night eating symptoms. What best explains her plateau?
options: Deficient thermic effect of food as primary cause
Inaccurate dietary recall with underestimated intake
items.explanation: Dietary recall is notoriously inaccurate; overweight individuals underestimate intake more than lean adults. Self-monitoring is needed.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Dietary recall is notoriously inaccurate (thus, Answer C is correct)."

items.question: The same patient has never kept a detailed food record. What intervention is most appropriate before escalating to pharmacotherapy?
options: Two-week comprehensive self-monitoring of food intake
Assumption that reported intake is accurate
items.explanation: A 2-week food record educates patients about true intake and often improves awareness and weight.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "This patient may not have tried to self-monitor food intake and thus should be provided the opportunity to do so."

items.question: A 34-year-old woman gained 40 lb over 2 years with hypertriglyceridemia and acne. She uses amitriptyline, depot medroxyprogesterone, and Tylenol PM; cortisol axis is normal. Which medications should be reconsidered?
options: Amitriptyline, depot medroxyprogesterone, and Tylenol PM
None—normal cortisol excludes iatrogenic weight gain
items.explanation: All three agents are associated with weight gain; Cushing syndrome is excluded but iatrogenic causes remain.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: A, B, and C) Tricyclic antidepressant amitriptyline she takes to treat neuralgia and depression and depot medroxyprogesterone she uses for contraception and Tylenol PM she takes 5 times per week to help fall asleep"

items.question: A patient walks 3 miles daily commuting. According to the chapter, approximate daily energy expenditure from this walking is:
items.explanation: Online walking calculators are accurate; 3 miles/day ≈300 kcal. Human efficiency is 20–30%, not 5–50%.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "This woman walks at least 3 miles per day to get to and from work, which will expend 300 kcal/day."

items.question: The thermic effect of food typically accounts for what fraction of ingested energy?
items.explanation: TEF averages 10% with a 2–20% range—too small to explain large energy balance errors.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The thermic effect of food (TEF) (Answer D) averages only 10% of ingested energy, with a range from 2% to 20% in our hands."

items.question: Which feature is required to diagnose night eating syndrome?
options: Evening hyperphagia or nocturnal ingestion with patient awareness and recall
items.explanation: Patients must be aware of and able to recall eating episodes; ≥3 additional criteria are needed.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The diagnosis requires that the patient be aware of and be able to recall the eating episodes."

items.question: Which cognitive behavioral strategy is most strongly linked to long-term weight-loss maintenance?
options: Weekly weighing only without food records
Relying on dietary recall at annual visits
items.explanation: Self-monitoring—especially of food—is the cornerstone of maintenance.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The cognitive behavioral technique most strongly associated with long-term, successful weight-loss maintenance is self-monitoring."

items.question: Which medications are noted for greatest weight gain via H1-receptor affinity?
items.explanation: Neuroleptics with highest H1R affinity (olanzapine, clozapine) cause the greatest weight gain.
items.reference: Case 2: "neuroleptics with highest affinity for the H1R (olanzapine and clozapine) are associated with the greatest weight gain."

items.question: After discontinuing amitriptyline and depot medroxyprogesterone, what outcome should be anticipated?
options: Weight loss is not guaranteed but substitution to weight-neutral agents may help
items.explanation: Merely stopping weight-promoting medications does not guarantee weight loss.
items.reference: Case 2: "Unfortunately, merely stopping these medications is no guarantee of weight loss."

items.question: Who is best suited to detailed diet history and motivational interviewing for obesity?
options: Dietitians with obesity management and behavioral training
items.explanation: Dietitians trained in motivational interviewing and obesity management are best suited when physicians lack time.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Dietitians with additional training in motivational interviewing, behavioral strategies, and obesity management are best suited for this task."

items.question: Who underestimates dietary intake to the greatest extent on recall?
options: Normal-weight adults more than those with obesity
People with overweight or obesity more than normal-weight adults
items.explanation: Overweight and obese individuals underestimate intake more than normal-weight adults.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "those who are overweight or obese underestimate to a greater extent than do normal-weight adults."

items.question: Besides structured walking, which activity category may contribute 100–700 kcal/day in the case discussion?
items.explanation: Housework as spontaneous activity can materially affect total daily expenditure.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "her housework, which may be characterized as spontaneous physical activity, can contribute as little as 100 to as much as 700 kcal/day"

items.question: What should providers avoid reinforcing about weight-loss diets?
options: That only very low-calorie diets can succeed
That moderate sustainable changes can work
items.explanation: Providers should avoid supporting the belief that only very restrictive diets succeed.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Providers are encouraged to avoid supporting patient contentions that only very low-calorie diets are successful."

items.statement: Human exercise efficiency typically ranges from 20% to 30% of oxygen consumption relative to work output.
items.explanation: The narrow efficiency range makes walking expenditure calculators reliable.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Humans have a relatively narrow range of exercise efficiency, ranging from 20% to 30% of oxygen consumption relative to work output"

items.statement: Dietary recall is accurate enough to guide weight-loss plans without self-monitoring.
items.explanation: Dietary recall is notoriously inaccurate; real-time monitoring is needed.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Dietary recall is notoriously inaccurate (thus, Answer C is correct)."

items.statement: A very low thermic effect of food can fully explain failure to lose weight on a perceived 1200 kcal/day diet.
items.explanation: Maximum TEF deviation is ~94 kcal/day—insufficient to explain the discrepancy.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "which cannot account for her failure to lose weight on a perceived intake of 1200 kcal/day."

items.statement: Night eating syndrome affects approximately 1.5% of the US general population.
items.explanation: Prevalence estimate is 1.5% in the general US population.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "the prevalence of night eating syndrome is 1.5% in the general population in the United States."

items.statement: Amitriptyline is associated with significant weight gain.
items.explanation: Older antidepressants used for pain, including amitriptyline, promote weight gain.
items.reference: Case 2: "amitriptyline (Answer A), are associated with significant weight gain."

items.statement: Depot medroxyprogesterone is associated with fat gain.
items.explanation: Depot medroxyprogesterone is linked to fat gain.
items.reference: Case 2: "depot medroxyprogesterone (Answer B) is associated with fat gain."

items.statement: Diphenhydramine in sleep aids can contribute to weight gain.
items.explanation: Older antihistamines including diphenhydramine are associated with weight gain.
items.reference: Case 2: "diphenhydramine (present in sleep aids because of its sedating properties) (Answer C) are also associated with weight gain."

items.statement: Attempting to record food intake for 2 weeks may itself increase dietary awareness and promote weight loss.
items.explanation: The act of recording often increases awareness and can produce weight loss.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "the mere act of trying to record what they are eating causes them to become more aware of their behavior and they lose weight"

items.assertion: Assertion: Self-monitoring is key to long-term weight-loss maintenance.
items.reason: Reason: Most people accurately recall their food intake without recording.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; inaccurate recall makes the reason false.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Dietary recall is notoriously inaccurate"

items.assertion: Assertion: Online walking energy calculators are quite accurate.
items.reason: Reason: Human exercise efficiency varies widely from 5% to 80%.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; efficiency is narrow (20–30%), not 5–80%.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Humans have a relatively narrow range of exercise efficiency, ranging from 20% to 30%"

items.assertion: Assertion: Appetite-stimulating medications make lifestyle weight loss harder.
items.reason: Reason: Medications that increase appetite oppose sustained energy deficit.
items.explanation: Both are true and causally linked.
items.reference: Case 2: "if patients are taking medications that increase appetite, it is more difficult to succeed with a comprehensive lifestyle weight-loss program."

items.assertion: Assertion: Exogenous glucocorticoids can increase appetite.
items.reason: Reason: They suppress hypothalamic POMC/α-MSH and unmask NPY/AgRP appetite drive.
items.explanation: Both are true with described mechanism.
items.reference: Case 2: "Exogenous corticosteroids suppress proopiomelanocortin, and thereby α-MSH production, resulting in an unopposed neuropeptide Y/agouti-related peptide effect to stimulate appetite."

items.assertion: Assertion: Night eating syndrome can be ruled out if the patient denies nocturnal eating with awareness.
items.reason: Reason: Night eating always occurs without patient recall.
items.explanation: Assertion is appropriate when criteria absent; unconscious eating contradicts diagnostic requirements.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The diagnosis requires that the patient be aware of and be able to recall the eating episodes."

items.assertion: Assertion: H1-receptor antagonists are associated with increased food intake.
items.reason: Reason: Hypothalamic histamine stimulates appetite via H1R.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; histamine inhibits appetite—the reason reverses physiology.
items.reference: Case 2: "Hypothalamic histamine suppresses feeding behavior via the histamine-1 receptor (H1R)"

items.assertion: Assertion: Stopping weight-gain medications may not produce weight loss.
items.reason: Reason: Weight gained on medications always reverses immediately upon discontinuation.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; immediate guaranteed reversal is false.
items.reference: Case 2: "Unfortunately, merely stopping these medications is no guarantee of weight loss."

items.assertion: Assertion: Moderate sustainable dietary changes are preferable to repeated very-low-calorie attempts.
items.reason: Reason: Only very-low-calorie diets have evidence for long-term success.
items.explanation: Assertion reflects chapter guidance; exclusive VLCD success is false.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Providers are encouraged to avoid supporting patient contentions that only very low-calorie diets are successful."
